retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Exercise
retrieved_docs.content: The AHA/ACC guidelines also recommend incorporating resistance strength training into regular physical activity, as this can help improve physical function and ability to exercise. [102] [118]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Exercise
retrieved_docs.content: According to the National Health Interview Survey, only 20.9% of adults met the 2008 federal physical activity guidelines for aerobic and strengthening activity. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Diet
retrieved_docs.content: The American Heart Association recommends the replacement of saturated fat with polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fats. [102] A 5% exchange in saturated fat consumption with polyunsaturated fat is associated with a 10% lower CAD risk (RR 0.90, 95% CI 0.83-0.97). [18] [103] As noted above, a 2018 review, however, challenged the strength of the traditional link between saturated fat and higher CAD risk compared to other nutrients. [39] In a separate review, there was a lack of a significant association between saturated fat and cardiovascular disease due to studies replacing saturated fat with highly refined carbohydrates. Coronary heart disease would be reduced if polyunsaturated fats replaced saturated fats. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Principles of Causation -- Summary / Explanation -- Sufficient and Component Cause Model
retrieved_docs.content: Strength of a cause: Determined by the prevalence of its components. A rare factor can be strong if its complementary causes are common. Interactions among causes: Mutual biological interactions exist within a sufficient cause. Induction period: The time from causal action to disease initiation. Latent period: The interval between disease occurrence and its detection. Synergism: The combined effect of causes exceeds the sum of their individual effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD): A 2017 meta-analysis noted that IBD is associated with a higher risk of CAD. However, the results were interpreted with caution due to the heterogeneity of the studies. The risk mechanism was uncertain, but it was thought to be due to a chronic inflammatory state. [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Stratification, Cardiac (Archived) -- Function -- Cardiovascular Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for cardiovascular disease can be separated into nonmodifiable and modifiable risk factors. These risk factors have been shown to have a cumulative effect over time in that the progression of CVD is related to the number of risk factors present and their persistence over time. More simply, the risk of developing CVD increases with the number of risk factors acquired and the time they have been present. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Stratification, Cardiac (Archived) -- Function -- Cardiovascular Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The remaining risk factors are modifiable and can be further categorized into health conditions and lifestyle factors. Health conditions include hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, and chronic kidney disease (CKD). Lifestyle factors include cigarette smoking, diet and exercise, obesity, and psychosocial factors. Hypertension has an abundant amount of evidence to support its role as a risk factor in developing CVD. A meta-analysis published in 2002 shows that blood pressure is “strongly and directly related to vascular (and overall) mortality,” showing that a difference of 20 mmHg of systolic or 10 mm Hg of diastolic pressure is associated with a nearly twofold difference in cardiovascular disease death rate. [7] Dyslipidemia, defined as elevated total or LDL cholesterol, low HDL cholesterol, and disturbances in lipoprotein metabolism, has been independently associated with CVD. [8] [9] Diabetes mellitus is considered a coronary heart disease (CHD) equivalent [10] secondary to the finding that patients with diabetes only had a similar risk of myocardial infarction compared to those without diabetes but with a prior myocardial infarction. [11] Chronic kidney disease is now also considered a CHD equivalent, just as diabetes mellitus is. Secondary to both proteinuria and a decreased glomerular filtration rate, it was shown that patients with even moderate renal impairment based on GFR and increased proteinuria were associated with an increase in all-cause and cardiovascular mortality compared to those with normal renal function. [12] Cigarette smoking is associated with a higher risk of myocardial infarction and is related to the amount used; however, the benefits of cessation are significant regardless of the amount or length of use. One study showed that smokers who stopped smoking had a risk similar to that of a nonsmoker within two years of quitting in a population of patients who had suffered a prior myocardial infarction. [13] Diets high in red meat and high-fat dairy products have been associated with having a higher risk of CHD. [14] A risk reduction was shown with diets high in fiber and daily consumption of fruits and vegetables. Exercise has been shown to reduce CHD risk and overall cardiovascular mortality. Obesity was shown to have a direct relationship with CVD risk. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Relative Risk -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Absolute risk is the actual risk of some event, given the current exposure. For example, if 1 in 10 individuals with exposure develops the disease, then the absolute risk of developing the disease with exposure is 10% or 1:10. If only 1 in 100 individuals without exposure develop the disease, then the absolute risk for developing the disease without exposure would be 1% or 1:100. Thus the relative risk of developing the disease would be 0.1 / 0.01 = 10. Therefore, an individual has a 10% chance of developing the disease with exposure (absolute risk), a 1% chance of developing the disease without exposure (absolute risk), and they are 10 times more likely to develop the disease if they have exposure (relative risk). [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Relative Risk -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Some confuse the relative risk with the odds ratio and absolute risk. Relative risk is the ratio of the probability of an event occurring with an exposure versus the probability of the event occurring without the exposure. Thus, to calculate the relative risk, we must know the exposure status of all individuals (either exposed or not exposed). This implies that relative risk is only appropriate for cases where the exposure status and disease incidence can be accurately determined, such as prospective cohort studies. [1] [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension: About 1 out of every 3 patients have hypertension. Hypertension and smoking were responsible for the largest number of deaths in a 2009 review comparing twelve modifiable risk factors. [20] Yet, only 54% of these patients achieve adequate blood pressure control. [21] Hypertension has long been a major risk factor for heart disease through both oxidative and mechanical stress it places on the arterial wall. [22] [23] A 1996 article reported that in the Framingham cohort, a systolic of 20 mmHg and diastolic of 10 mmHg increase was observed from age 30 to 65 years. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern -- Socioeconomic Status
retrieved_docs.content: Socioeconomic status is a significant risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Upstream determinants include financial strain, lack of affordable and nutritious food, exposure to domestic violence, and inadequate housing; this is an important consideration to consider, given existing cardiovascular disease risk equations do not capture this. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2186
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) and with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني). It also commonly co‑occurs with vascular stiffness (رأب الوعاء). These interconnections indicate that patients with coronary artery disease often have underlying cardiac abnormalities, are at heightened risk for developing congestive heart failure, and frequently exhibit vascular stiffness, all of which are important considerations for diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a disorder of kidney function (اختلال وظيفة الكلى) and includes specific forms such as chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, indicating that hypertension can directly cause this type of renal impairment. Chronic kidney disease is associated with multiple risk factors (rizikové faktory), and recognizing and controlling these factors—particularly hypertension—is essential for preventing disease progression and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1647
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is linked to ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال, indicating a possible association between this vascular hardening disorder and that specific condition. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is associated with several observable findings: the presence of ال​نفخة الانقباضية (a systolic murmur), لغط الشريان السباتي (a carotid bruit), and the need for حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance) to monitor and manage disease progression. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype, التصلب العصيدي المعمم, which represents a more generalized form of the disease. Pharmacologically, the PCSK9 inhibitor alirocumab may prevent the development or progression of التصلب العصيدي, suggesting a therapeutic role for lipid‑lowering strategies in patients at risk for this condition. Together, these relationships highlight the clinical manifestations, related conditions, disease subtypes, and potential preventive treatment options relevant to the management of التصلب العصيدي.

From a systemic perspective, the condition “Risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion” is associated with peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which maps to aortic calcification, highlighting the link between systemic atherosclerotic disease and localized cardiac calcific changes. The same risk condition is also associated with the atrial mucous tumor, indicating a potential cerebrovascular complication linked to this specific atrial pathology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how chronic cardiac diseases, structural heart lesions, systemic vascular risk factors, diagnostic and therapeutic cardiac procedures, device management strategies, and associated clinical conditions are interwoven, providing a structured framework essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate device therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1501
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي), and coronary artery atherosclerosis is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي). This interconnection suggests that individuals with a cardiac disorder may be at increased risk for developing coronary artery disease, and that coronary artery disease may coexist with or predispose to rheumatic heart disease, underscoring the need for comprehensive cardiovascular assessment and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is characterized by hemodynamic abnormalities such as elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure, both of which are direct manifestations of the condition. In clinical practice, essential hypertension can progress to a hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), a severe escalation that is mapped to the underlying essential hypertension. Additionally, essential hypertension is clinically associated with gout (مرض النقرس), indicating that patients with this form of high blood pressure have an increased likelihood of developing or co‑existing with gout. Recognizing these interrelated features—blood pressure elevations, the potential for crisis, and the link to gout—helps clinicians assess risk, monitor disease progression, and tailor comprehensive management strategies for affected individuals.
